data.title: MongoDB: Sell Before The Weak Fundamentals Catch Up With The Stock (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Thesis

MongoDB, Inc. (NASDAQ:MDB) stock has gained around 20% in the past six months, but is still down more than 35% for the past year as investors look for a rebound. In the below analysis, I determine that the current technicals are strong in their outlook for the stock. However, as I analyze the fundamentals, I find that revenue growth is decelerating and their guidance for FY2025 Q4 implies that it will further decelerate in the coming quarter. Guidance also shows that adjusted EPS growth is expected to fall into negative territory. For the valuation, while the P/S ratio is at low levels relative to the past three years, it still fails to reflect the upcoming slowdown in growth and is at a major premium to peers. Therefore, I believe it would be wise to take advantage of the technical strength to sell MDB stock before the reality of the deteriorating growth sets in.

Technicals

Charting

Trends

MDB stock broke out of its longer-term uptrend back in mid-December. That uptrend dated back to late August, but this trend line has now become resistance after the breakdown. The stock has, however, developed a new nearer term uptrend since early January and since its slope is steeper than the former uptrend line, the stock has a chance of reentering the longer uptrend at some point.

Support

In addition to the nearer term uptrend line, there are two other significant support levels. The first is in the mid-250s and represents major support in early October and resistance in early January. The other support area is in the low 230s. This price level acted as key support in late August as well as at the end of December.

Resistance

Other than the former uptrend line, there are two more noteworthy resistance levels. The nearer one is in the high 290s, very near the current stock price, and was resistance from September to mid-November. The other resistance area is at 350 and represents a major downside gap. This level was also resistance in late November and since the volume was overwhelming on the day of the gap, its importance is high.

Takeaway

I would say that this chart is a net positive one for MDB. The stock has developed a new uptrend and has hopes of reclaiming the longer-term uptrend line. However, there is quite a balance of support and resistance levels, and so the stock's movement maybe slightly limited.

Moving Averages

Simple Moving Average (SMA)

The 50-day SMA has remained below the 200-day SMA for the past six months, showing sustained bearishness for the longer term. However, the gap between the SMAs decreased significantly from August to December and is currently holding stable, showing that bearish momentum has receded and has been highly contained.

Exponential Moving Average (EMA)

The 9 day EMA had a bullish crossover with the 21 day EMA in late January, an indication of near term strength. The gap between the EMAs has expanded at a slow rate, reflecting a gradual increase in near term bullish momentum. The stock is now trading above both the EMAs and SMAs and so the stock is in a solid technical position currently.

Bollinger Bands

The stock broke back above the midline in mid-January, another signal of strength. This signal is also a confirmation of the nearer term uptrend, and the midline should hold as support moving forward if the uptrend is a strong one. The stock is currently relatively near the upper band but is not there yet, showing that it's not overbought despite the recent rally.

Takeaway

I believe these moving averages are net positive in their signalling. The SMAs show that longer term bearish momentum has receded significantly, the EMAs show accelerating near term bullishness, and the Bollinger Bands provided confirmation of the start of the nearer term uptrend in the stock.

Indicators

Moving Average Convergence Divergence (MACD)

The MACD has a bullish crossover with the signal line in early January. After the crossover, the gap between the lines expanded quite a bit as shown by the histogram but in the past few weeks, the gap has had some periods of expansion and contraction. The histogram has been mixed as of late showing that bullishness has been a bit unstable.

Relative Strength Index (RSI)

The RSI currently is at 66.15 after it reclaimed the 50 level in mid-January as a sign that the bulls have reclaimed control from the bears. The RSI has been on an uptrend of its own since the late December low in the indicator, and so strength may continue to build for the stock moving forward.

Stochastics

Overall, the stochastics is in a bullish position currently. At the start of the new year, there was a major bullish crossover as the %K crossed above the %D. Since then, there have been bearish crossovers that turned out to be mainly false signals that reflect only a pause in bullish momentum rather than a reversal. There has been an abundance of crossovers lately, but currently, since lines are within the 80+ zone, the stochastics reflects that the bulls are in firm control of the stock.

Takeaway

As a whole, these indicators were fairly bullish in their outlook for the stock. The MACD is still in a bullish state overall, the RSI is in a promising strengthening trend, and the stochastics is in the 80+ zone. Therefore, further technical strength is likely at this point.

Fundamentals

Earnings

Quarterly Results

MDB is expected to release their FY2025 Q4 results on March 5th. They released their Q3 earnings back in December and showed mixed results overall. They reported total revenues of $529.4 million, up 22% YoY and a net loss per share of $0.13 versus a $0.41 loss in the year ago period. As shown in the above chart, revenue growth is at resilient levels but overall, despite some improvement over the past quarter, it is at below average levels and is still in a worrying downtrend. For EPS, Q3 represented the narrowest net loss per share in the past three years and showed great improvement both YoY and QoQ, and so there are promising signs. EPS also seems to be in an uptrend and so the company has the potential of breaking even, moving forward. In non-GAAP terms, EPS increased from $0.96 to $1.16 YoY, reflecting a 20.8% growth rate. All of these results beat expectations with revenue beating by $33.65 million, GAAP EPS beating by $0.72 and adjusted EPS beating by $0.47, showing that performance versus consensus estimates was strong.

Guidance

MDB provided guidance for their FY2025 Q4. They expect revenues to be between $515 million and $519 million. In their FY2024 Q4, they reported revenues of $458 million, and so this guidance implies a revenue growth rate of between 12.4% and 13.3%. This is a major slowdown from Q3 and shows that the downtrend continues in revenue growth, a worrying sign. They also project that non-GAAP EPS will be between $0.62 and $0.65 for their Q4 and since they had a non-GAAP EPS of $0.86 in FY2024 Q4, this guidance implies adjusted EPS growth of between -24.4% and -27.9%. This is a major difference from Q3's YoY growth of above 20% and shows that the outlook is very concerning for MDB. Overall, I believe both the revenue and EPS guidance are worrying as they imply slowing growth and a plunge into negative growth, respectively.

Valuation

Price To Sales Ratio (P/S)

The P/S ratio is currently at very low levels in relation to the past three years of data. It has a reading of 11.21 as of now and has climbed somewhat to start the new year. Despite this relatively low level in the multiple, I still believe MDB stock is overvalued. As discussed above, revenue growth is currently in a downtrend and quite near three-year lows. Furthermore, the revenue guidance that they provided implies a major growth slowdown coming in their Q4. The implied growth of between 12.4% and 13.3% is much below Q3's growth rate of 22%. It would also likely be the worst revenue growth recorded by MDB in the past three years. Therefore, while the P/S ratio is at low levels, I believe it's not low enough as it doesn't reflect this slowdown sufficiently. This disconnect leads me to believe that the stock is overvalued relative to growth. Currently, the Information Technology sector's median P/S ratio is only 3.36 and so MDB is trading at an over 200% premium to peers. Given the deteriorating growth outlook for the company, this premium is hard to justify currently. Therefore, I conclude that MDB is overvalued at current levels, as the P/S ratio is at risk of contraction as the reality of the fundamentals sink in.

Conclusion

In the above analysis, I have determined that the technicals are strong for the stock. Instead of believing that investors should buy into this strength, I believe investors should use this as a selling opportunity. As discussed above, the fundamentals of the business are becoming concerning as revenue growth deceleration is expected to continue and adjusted EPS growth is projected to drop into deeply negative territory. While the P/S ratio is at relatively low levels compared to its history, I believe it still doesn't adequately show the concerning direction for the company. Therefore, I believe a sell rating is warranted, as investors should use the strong technicals to sell MDB as the fundamentals deteriorate.

data.title: Federal Reserve Watch: Trump Starts To Move
data.description:
The Federal Reserve has been dealing with the fact that the current monetary situation began with a period in which the Fed pumped lots and lots of money into the banking system, producing a very rapid growth in the money stock, followed by a period of almost three years of quantitative tightening which has resulted in a very modest rise in the money stock.

Overall, during the past five years, the M2 stock of money has risen at a compound rate of growth of 7.0 percent from December 30, 2019 to December 23, 2024.

Here is what the chart looks like.

So, over the past five years, the Federal Reserve has put plenty of money into the economic system.

And, this is why the Federal Reserve is worried about the United States becoming more inflated.

Since March 2022, the Federal Reserve has been conducting a monetary policy of quantitative tightening.

That is, since the middle of March 2022, the Federal Reserve has been reducing the size of the Fed's securities portfolio, reducing the amount of excess reserves in the commercial banking system.

Here is a picture of this activity.

In pursuing its monetary "tightening" in this way, the Federal Reserve has been able to reduce the amount of excess reserves in the commercial banking system so as to reduce the threat of future inflation, while at the same time allowing the economy to continue to grow in the range of 2.00%-2.50%.

This program has resulted in a falling rate of inflation while at the same time keeping the unemployment rate near a 50-year low.

Overall, the result of this approach has kept the economy in a pretty good position.

But, there still remain lots and lots of excess reserves in the banking system. In other words, the Federal Reserve still has a long way to go to get the banking system back to a more stable position, one that does not contain all the excess money which could cause further serious rounds of inflation in the American economy.

The Federal Reserve has slowed down the rate at which it is reducing the size of the securities portfolio. This adjustment came about in the middle of September.

Note that this adjustment to the pace of the reduction in the securities portfolio appears to have come as Federal Reserve officials moved to reduce the level of its policy rate of interest...the Federal Funds rate.

Here is the picture of the upper bound of the policy rate of interest.

So, since the middle of September 2024, the Federal Reserve has reduced the level of its policy rate of interest three times, the last move coming in December 2024.

Recently, the Fed passed on lowering its policy rate of interest another notch as it did not believe that further movements were justified at this time given the current state of the economy and the monetary system.

Analysts seem to believe that maybe the Federal Reserve has reached the lowest level it is going to reach this year for the policy rate.

As I have been writing, inflationary expectations have seemed to pick up in the financial markets, working against any "new" changes in the level of the interest rates.

With these inflationary expectations apparently moving to become even higher, the justification of further decreases in the policy rate of interest seem very doubtful at this time.

However, there remains the need to continue to reduce the size of the Fed's securities portfolio.

On March 16, 2022, the size of the Fed's securities portfolio was right around $8.5 trillion. This is up from a size of $3.8 trillion on March 20, 2019.

Now, on February 12, 2025, the size of the securities portfolio was $6.5 trillion.

So, the Fed's buildup added $4.7 trillion to the securities portfolio.

And, due to the quantitative tightening, the Fed has removed $2.0 trillion but still has a ways to go before getting back near the level of the portfolio before the Covid-19 pandemic hit.

As far as the policy rate of interest goes, the Fed has been working it down as it policy considerations dictate.

The Federal Reserve has a pathway that it has been following, and, so far, it has done a very good job, I believe, in staying on the path and bringing the Fed's balance sheet closer to where it needs to be.

But, I argue, the Fed still has a relatively long way to go.

Now, however, Fed officials have another problem.

The current U.S. president wants the Federal Reserve to follow another path. The path President Trump wants the Fed to follow is one of lower interest rates, with more financial stimulus to get the U.S. economy growing even faster.

Right now, if the Federal Reserve were to increase purchases of government securities to increase the size of the Fed's securities portfolio and reduce the Fed's policy rate of interest the major result would be a faster acceleration of the inflation rate.

My point is...there is already a substantial excess of "cash" in the banking system due to previous Federal Reserve action to combat the Covid-19 pandemic.

To do what Trump wants the Fed to do at this time would be just to pile excesses upon existing excesses.

And, I believe, that expectations in the commercial banking system are such that these expectations will explode.

The commercial banking system is already overloaded with excess cash!

To move to pump up the banking system further to drive down interest rates would only pump in more cash on top of the cash that is already there.

But, this seems to be the battle that is brewing...Trump versus Powell and the current Federal Reserve Board.

How this confrontation is resolved is going to have a lot to do with where interest rates, where the banking system, where the financial markets, where the price level, and where the economy goes.

The Trump monetary policy, one to keep real estate markets booming, is a policy for inflation. It always has been. It always will be.

Watch.

data.title: Markets That Shrug Off Bad News Want Higher (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Market Analysis - Nasdaq (NASDAQ:QQQ)

I wrote last week that the market had been gappy and choppy, but that it was putting in a pattern of higher highs and higher lows, that it was starting to form the kind of flag pattern we'd ordinarily buy if it were a stock.

The gappy, volatile nature of the market was giving me fits - as a momentum trader, you want smoother moves after all - but I went back to the basics and looked carefully at the pattern of higher highs and higher lows. As long as those are in place, it tells you there is some sort of bid under the market.

This week, we got just what I was looking for as the market pushed up and out of this wedge pattern. I also noted a subtle change in character as the market was significantly less gappy. The only large gap, in fact, a -1% open on Wednesday in response to an above expectations CPI print, was bought immediately and aggressively by market makers all day.

Markets that shrug off bad news while trading up towards all-time highs are strong markets. And we had a decent amount of net negative news this week between the back and forth statements on tariffs from the President and internationally as well as the previously mentioned hot CPI report.

Markets that shrug off such news are markets that want higher. Bull markets climb a wall of worry.

Daily chart on the left, weekly on the right:

From a very short-term perspective, we are a little extended from moving averages with all-time highs on the left side acting as potential resistance (light gray line). It wouldn't surprise me to see the market chop around a little bit somewhere in the realm of those highs before pushing higher.

But, from an intermediate term perspective, this is a market I expect to trend higher, and I will position as such until it proves me wrong.

As I have noted repeatedly over the last weeks and months, I generally have a bullish bias right now, especially with an extremely pro business Trump Administration in place. Yes, there will be inevitable corrections. Yes, the odds do favor a risk event or panic at some point. But if this market tells me it wants to move higher, and we have the bullish political backdrop, I am going to do my best to ride that wave.

I watch the market closely to try to glean clues about the overall environment for taking new swing and momentum positions. Bad environments mean a lot of volatility or outright downward pressure and thus a higher likelihood of running stops on new positions where I do not yet have cushion. Good environments mean we have a trending market where new positions are more likely to immediately start working, allowing me to trail them for weeks or months against the moving averages.

Within that framework, this week’s action suggests to me, it's time to position more aggressively although with careful risk management and stops. As always, if the market fakes us out and turns back down, we do run the risk of hitting those stops, but on balance, at the start of a potential market trend, the greater risk is to be sitting on the sidelines.

Mid Cap Growth (IWP)

I follow the IWP because it correlates well with the smaller, growthier names I prefer to trade. These are the kinds of stocks that tend to have the most potential in a trending market, making bigger and faster moves than their large cap counterparts.

The IWP looks healthy. I noted last week that I saw some signs of short-term exhaustion in the realm of all-time highs. Sure enough, the IWP traded down to the 21 EMA (teal line) to start the week before reclaiming the 10 and 21 EMAs (pink and teal line), working off some of that extension and exhaustion.

To my eyes, this looks like it is ready to move higher.

Bitcoin (BTC)

While I am excited by what I see from the QQQ and IWP, to my eyes, Bitcoin seems fairly directionless while trading around in this larger range (light grey line overhead, light blue line underneath).

Long term, I have a bullish bias for Bitcoin. In the short and intermediate term, I need price to declare itself and tell me if it is more interested in breaking out to the upside or to the downside.

For now, I can't take anything actionable from this chart and will be setting Bitcoin, crypto, and its related stocks to the side although will keep it on my radar. I am not really going to do anything while it chops around in this box.

The next catalyst would be whatever Trump or his crypto czar decides to do.

It is worth noting that Trump's meme coin is down about 70% from highs:

As I have said before, it is an absolute, total, and complete disgrace that a President would pump and dump a meme coin onto his followers.

That said, I have trouble believing he is just going to set this to the side and watch it go to zero. It still has a 4.5 billion dollar market cap as of this writing. He has to know that if he gets the crypto market to get moving, his meme coin will inevitably do well too. I trade prices, but not psychology, but it is worth keeping in the back of mind that Trump has a multi-billion dollar reason to get crypto moving at some point.

Portfolio

I am currently sitting in more names than I would like and plan to cull the laggards in the coming week or two while adding to the winners if they give me good buy points.

Longs: Oklo (OKLO), CrowdStrike (CRWD), Tempus AI (TEM), Grail Inc. (GRAL), Sea Ltd. (SE), BigBear.ai (BBAI), Palantir (PLTR), Affirm (AFRM), Digital Turbine (APPS), Roblox (RBLX), Doximity (DOCS), Peloton (PTON), Shopify (SHOP), Innodata (INOD), DraftKings (DKNG), Archer Aviation (ACHR), Snowflake (SNOW).

Sadly, I sold Reddit (RDDT) after a bad earnings reaction after a 200%+ gain from my initial buy point. I do think we are not quite done with Reddit as a viable trading stock. My suspicion is that it will need a few weeks or months to consolidate after the crazy tear it went on in 2024 and to digest the earnings report, after which I will probably be revisiting it.

Story Stocks

In a hot market, you'll often find the companies that are on theme and sell a great story really moving. Yes, many of these are glorified science projects. Yes, many of them will be trading down 80% in the next year or two. No, that doesn't really matter to me if I can ride it to big gains. (For a longer discussion on this, please check out this article under the heading Destiny Tech100 (DXYZ), honestly this was probably one of the more important things I've written about momentum trading and bull markets.)

And the gains have been there if you are following themes that are moving and accumulation volume patterns. FYI - this is why I keep going back to the Quantum stocks to see if they have another leg in them.

OKLO is up about 90% from the buy point. I have discussed the nuclear theme and the AI power provider theme many times.

To my eyes, it looks like it wants to chop more or less sideways into earnings. My stops are trending on the 10 and 21 EMAs, and I will otherwise trim heavily into earnings in case there is a bad reaction.

BigBear.ai (BBAI) recently reported a DoD Contract to advance its Virtual Anticipation Network Prototype. It is up about 96% from the gap and go buy point on that news:

GRAL, maker of the incredibly exciting Galleri cancer detection product, is also up ~91% from the buy point:

Tempus AI (TEM), sitting at the intersection of AI and healthcare, and Digital Turbine (APPS), have also each done exceptionally well.

I'm not saying all this to brag about my winners. I've run stops in other names just like anyone else. Instead, I am saying this to point out the unbelievable power of focusing on the "in play" stocks in the hottest themes in a good market.

Watch for hot themes. Monitor for accumulation volume patterns. Find a tight consolidation area. Then buy on a breakout through that area. Simple but definitely not easy.

I am going to continue to watch for story stocks and theme stocks with big moves and big volume until the market tells me it won't work anymore.

Affirm (AFRM)

AFRM was probably the buy I was most excited about in the last week or two. I think the chart really speaks for itself.

In the weekly chart on the right, you can see that this thing completely fell apart after the 2020-2021 bubble and has been dead money for years, capped by that ~51.5 level (light gray line).

Recently, however, you'll note a marked change in character with the volume showing accumulation - high volume on up weeks, low volume on pullback weeks - and with the stock breaking out of that 51.5 level.

From there, it pulled back to 51.5 in an orderly fashion, putting in a nice base, before breaking out up and to multiyear highs on the back of a fantastic earnings report.

This looks like a stock that has just entered a "Stage 2 Uptrend" (from Stan Weinstein, a brilliant technician) and if its history is any guide, it has the potential to be a big winner.

Watch List

As mentioned, I am looking to cull some names and size up in some I already own more than I am looking to add a bunch of new names at present.

Balanced with that, though, is that we appear to be in a hot and story-driven market... so I am going to keep a close eye on where the volume and the buzz is going and be prepared to deploy there.

Aside from "story stocks" or "theme stocks," here is what I have been focusing on:

2020-2021 Winners that Developed Real Businesses

I think we're going to see some money rotate into some of the names that hit peaks in the 2020-2021 bubble and have been dead money since.

From a fundamental standpoint, these companies were dumped onto retail investors and then went up like rocket ships despite having no real earnings. The thing is... some of them have actually gone on to build real businesses, have real and growing revenue, and are starting to actually make money.

One of the most powerful patterns in technical analysis is when a "dead money" stock starts to wake up. I discussed this up above when I mentioned Stan Weinstein's Stage 2 Uptrend pattern.

We have already seen this with AFRM as noted above, but also with Roblox (RBLX) (long):

Roblox shows virtually the same pattern. Bubble highs. Dead money capped by a level, before showing accumulation volume and pushing through that level.

Sea Ltd (SE) shows the same pattern (also long):

Not all of these are going to reach their prior highs... but some will, and when they do, I intend to be there.

Upstart (UPST) is a fast-growing company that has a similar pattern and is high on my watch list.

Upstart has already started making some noise, and personally, I think it is for real this time. It just released a well regarded earnings report and has issued strong guidance. Look at the huge volume in that last week after a lower volume pullback.

Snowflake shows a similar pattern. I have a small long position but would like to get more aggressive if it gives me the opportunity and shows it wants to move higher.

Uber (UBER)

Some people love him. Some people hate him. But I am, for one, a big fan of Bill Ackman, who is a brilliant investor.

Uber has been a rocket ship this week on the news that Bill Ackman has taken a large stake in the company. This feels to me like the type of company that every growth oriented mutual fund is going to be stashing on their books over the coming years.

More importantly for me, the chart looks really good, especially with the backdrop of strong earnings and revenue growth. It has consolidated in a nearly years long base under that ~82 level and has started waking up over on that right side. I'd like to see it consolidate and set a tight supply/demand level somewhere near that 82 level and would plan to buy on a breakout through it.

Conclusion

As always, we will see what the market actually has in store for us. That said, with the market shrugging off bad news over the last month and breaking out of a flag pattern range near all-time highs, I have a bullish bias.

I am most focused on "story stocks" and "theme stocks" as well as 2020-2021 winners that have used the last 4 years to actually develop a business.

Remember, risk comes first. Trading is nothing more than a game of, on average and over the balance of many trades, selling stocks for more in gains than you stop out of in losses… then recycling that edge over again and again. If you want to learn more, the rabbit hole starts with William O'Neill's How to Make Money in Stocks, which may open your eyes to an entirely new investing philosophy and could give you the foundation you need to start momentum trading.

data.title: LKQ Corporation: They've Got Parts, They've Got Scale, They've Got My Attention
data.description:
Summary

LKQ Corporation (NASDAQ:LKQ) is a relatively larger player in the automotive parts and services industry, known for its wide range of product offerings and robust operational network. In this note, we’ll take a deep dive into LKQ's business operations, financial performance and competitive advantages. Additionally, we’ll address valuation and potential catalysts that could shape LKQ’s share price going forward. Overall, there is a lot to love with this company and I think this is a Buy opportunity.

The key points of my thesis include:

  • Undervalued from a historic basis

  • Strong historical financial performance and balance sheet

  • Promising growth prospects based on a size and scale advantage

Business Overview and Company Insights

LKQ is a leading distributor of replacement parts, components, and systems for repairs and maintenance. In addition to OEM recycled parts, they also offer aftermarket parts. LKQ distributes these products to collision and mechanical repair shops, coupled with after market collision and mechanical products, recycled collision and mechanical products, etc.. LKQ has operations in North America, Europe and Taiwan. More detail can be found in the 10-K.

There Are Many Factors To Consider, But The Key Consideration Is Consumer Behavior

Vehicle Sales

The obvious indicator for LKQ is vehicle sales, as greater turnover of purchased vehicles increases demand for maintenance and repair parts.

Consumer Confidence

Consumer confidence impacts discretionary spending, and car repairs can be deferred during economic downturns.

Used Car Values

Lower used car prices make vehicles more likely to be totaled rather than repaired after accidents

Repair Costs

Rising labor and parts costs can lead to more vehicles being totaled rather than repaired

Vehicle Miles Traveled (VMT) and Accident Rates

LKQ's business is closely tied to the number of vehicles on the road and accident rates. Metrics such as VMT, accident frequency, and severity could impact topline revenue. According to data from the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA), VMT has been increasing in recent years, which could be a tailwind for LKQ.

LKQ Possesses A Wide Range Of Parts Availability + Distribution Capabilities That Make It Hard To Beat

Scale and Distribution Network

LKQ has built an unmatched distribution infrastructure that allows for superior service levels, particularly evident after the Uni-Select acquisition. They can deliver parts multiple times per day to body shops, compared to competitors who might only deliver once or twice a week. This frequency and reliability create stronger customer relationships.

Product Breadth and Availability

LKQ offers a comprehensive solution by providing aftermarket parts, salvage parts, and now paint products through a single distribution channel. This "one-stop shop" approach is particularly valuable for body shops looking to streamline their supplier relationships. Their scale also allows them to maintain deeper inventory levels than competitors.

Cost Structure Advantages

Due to size and integrated network, LKQ can spread fixed costs across a larger revenue base. For example, when they added paint distribution through FinishMaster, they could leverage existing delivery routes and warehouse infrastructure, making their cost-to-serve significantly lower than standalone paint distributors.

Financial Strength

Despite a high-ish debt to equity ratio, LKQ still has the financial flexibility to invest in growth, weather downturns, and make strategic acquisitions when opportunities arise. Smaller competitors often lack this financial flexibility, particularly during challenging market conditions like the current environment. You may have noticed from other companies I’ve chosen to write about, that more or less, the companies I choose to cover usually possess this ability based on how I screen.

Employee Satisfaction

Traditional wisdom suggests higher employee satisfaction leads to better performance. 75% of employees say it's a great place to work—significantly above the 57% U.S. company average. In addition, 70% of LKQ employees would recommend their employer to a friend. Taking these two data points combined, it seems like the majority of employees have a favorable attitude towards the company.

Revenues Are Growing At A Nice Clip

LKQ has demonstrated consistent revenue growth, from roughly ~$6.7B in 2014 to ~$13.9B in 2023. However there was some volatility during this timeframe, such as a significant decline in 2020 to $11.6B due to COVID, followed by a strong recovery in 2021 with revenues of $13.1B. Quarterly revenues have also shown steady progression, with the most recent quarter showing a y/y increase. This sustained growth reflects LKQ's resilience and ability to expand its market share.

Margins Have Grown Since COVID, But Are They Starting To Dip?

With some notable fluctuations, LKQ's operating and profit margins have improved consistently and gradually. Since COVID, LKQ's operating margin peaked at 11.27% in 2022. However, we've seen the operating margin decline slightly in 2023 and stabilize so far into 2024. Despite these minor blips, LKQ has maintained steady profitability, proving their ability to thrive in tough economic times.

3Q24 Earnings Call Highlights

5 Key Takeaways:

  • Adjusted diluted EPS of $0.88, up 2% from prior year

  • $341M in FCF for the quarter

  • Repurchased 3M shares for approximately $125M

  • Board approved additional $1B share repurchase authorization through October 2026

  • Lowered full year 2024 guidance

LKQ's 3Q24 earnings present a mixed picture with both challenges and spots of brilliance. The primary concern is the significant decline in North American repairable claims, which dropped 9.5% year-over-year and is worse than Q2's decline. Management attributes this to rising insurance premiums and falling used car prices, which are causing consumers to delay or forego repairs. While they expect these headwinds to persist into early 2025, they anticipate improvement in 2H25 as used car prices stabilise. On the bright side, there are several positive indicators. Despite the revenue challenges, LKQ maintained strong cash flow generation ($341M FCF in 3Q24) and demonstrated commitment to shareholder returns through both dividends and share repurchases. The Board's approval of an additional $1B share repurchase authorization signals management's belief that the stock is undervalued.

Analyzing Debt Trends: How Leverage Impacts Company Performance

LKQ’s debt to equity is admittedly pushing the limit of what I’m comfortable with. LKQ's spike in debt to equity ratio in 2019 and again in 2023 are particularly noteworthy. These increases are from strategic decisions such as M&A. While a moderate amount of debt can fuel expansion, excessive reliance on it can elevate financial risk. Going forward, we will monitor whether LKQ will reduce its debt. I sense that the ratio will likely remain relatively stable in the short term, but it is important to reduce leverage to allow for financial flexibility in downturns.

Dividends Are Growing But The Track Record Is Short

LKQ has only been consistently paying dividends for a relatively short period, but has shown growth in its dividend payments thus far. LKQ's ability to maintain and potentially grow its dividend is supported by strong fundamental factors, including consistent free cash flow generation ($661M year-to-date as of 3Q24), an investment-grade credit rating, and stable market positions across multiple segments. While LKQ's dividend history may be shorter than some industrial companies, management has established a track record of reliable payments and shown commitment to maintaining them through various market conditions.

Share Counts Are Starting To Decline, Nice

LKQ has reduced its share count in the past decade, showing a shareholder-friendly approach. 292M shares were outstanding in 2010, but that went down to 268M in 2023. Over time, share count has steadily declined. As of 3Q24, shares outstanding further decreased to 263M. This consistent reduction in shares outstanding suggests LKQ's ongoing commitment to share buyback programs and improving per-share metrics

Continued Restructurings, M&A, FX And High Interest Rates Will Challenge The Expense Line

Restructuring + Transaction Expenses

LKQ experienced higher restructuring and transaction-related expenses at $99M YTD through 9/30/24, compared to $53M in 2023. The increase is due to LKQ's 2024 Global Restructuring Plan. $70M of the $99M is tied to this plan, which includes the sale of operations in Slovenia, Poland, and Bosnia. At best, LKQ's restructuring efforts signal a move towards improved profitability.

Interest Expenses

LKQ also saw a significant rise in interest expenses, hitting $196M YTD through 9/30/24, up from $150M in 2023. This jump in interest expense is largely because of higher debt tied to the Uni-Select acquisition. The effect of rising interest expenses on LKQ's profitability warrants consideration. Given we are now in a rate cutting cycle, I don't see this type of expense to move significantly higher. However, rate is only one part of the equation. Investors should also pay attention to management's ability to manage its capital and funding needs.

FX Introducing Volatility Into The P&L

LKQ recorded a $49M gain fromFX contracts related to acquisitions YTD through 9/30/24.. The unpredictability of these FX impacts on the P&L make it difficult to account for in an earnings model, but we’ll try our best to take any hints from management where we can. Oddly enough, the foreign exchange gain almost completely offsets the elevated interest expense from the same transaction.

The Valuation Points Upward

My blended valuation of LKQ is ~$70 / share based on a 1-year forward look of earnings and free cash flow, coupled with the recent addition of using an industry avg PE. The line of thinking with the latter is that my belief is this company is performing better than average, so it should at least trade at a multiple that's higher than the industry average. Compared to currently traded levels at around $38 a share, there’s some significant upside here. The NTM earnings figure should bump up my estimate after I switch to adjusted EPS, as opposed to reported. But luckily, I have FCF as a fallback since these estimates aren’t precise.

Higher Than Street On Revenue, Tougher On Earnings

My FY24 top line estimates are more optimistic than Street, but my EPS estimates are significantly below. This will need to be fixed in the future, as I believe the Street is reporting an adjusted EPS figure, while I’m using the reported figure. Even if I discounted the Street’s figures, I’m still coming in on the low side, so my expense control estimates aren’t as optimistic. Overall, I believe that sales will still hold, particularly in the US as the economy is still very healthy with low unemployment figures that should continue to drive consumer spending in the next year or two.

A Recession Will Be The Key Headwind, Apart From That There IS Room to Run Higher

Auto Parts Industry Consolidation

LKQ has a history of successful acquisitions, and further consolidation could lead to more market share. LKQ can boost its position within its space by acquiring and integrating smaller players.

Used Vehicle Market

As the used car market continues to expand, driven by factors such as rising new car prices and changing consumer preferences, demand for aftermarket parts is likely to increase.

EVs Entering The Mix

LKQ is adapting to new tech and market trends, which was once a threat but is now a tailwind. LKQ can take advantage of the growing electric vehicle market by developing and distributing EV-specific replacement parts.

The Obvious Recession Risk

A recession could pose a significant risk to LKQ's business, as it would for pretty much any other business out there. The line of thinking is that consumers may be forced to cut back on discretionary spending, including vehicle repairs and maintenance. In challenging times, consumers may be forced to delay or forego non-essential repairs, leading to decreased demand for aftermarket parts and services. A counterargument to this is that individuals may opt to repair in place of purchasing a replacement vehicle.

Conclusion

Wrapping things up, I think LKQ is a solid business with strong fundamentals, a wide distribution network, and a track record of steady revenue growth. Management’s ability to navigate industry challenges is impressive. That said, the valuation offers enough margin of safety for me to be fully on board at current levels.

On a separate note, this marks my last initiation piece for a while. Covering ~10 companies solo is plenty, and I want to make sure I’m focusing on names that genuinely keep my interest. Reasons for me to drop a name include lack of interest, the company being acquired, going private or going bust. But for now, this is my steady-state coverage. Thanks for reading, and as always, I’ll be back with updates when there’s something worth talking about. Please keep up to date with my current coverage on social media and let me know your thoughts on LKQ by commenting below.

data.title: The Honest Company Is Overvalued But A Long-Term Buy
data.description:
Honest, You’ll Like It

We have great faith in the long-term sales and profit growth of personal care companies like The Honest Company (NASDAQ:HNST). For instance, we remain positive about Helen of Troy Ltd. (HELE) and The Procter & Gamble Company (PG), though the stock of the former is -52% and the stock of the more diversified business, P&G, is +9% over the past 12 months. Their market caps are in the billions of dollars. The shares of Honest are +102.8% this past year.

Honest is a niche player in the baby and personal care products markets. Its market cap is $606.7M. Following a 20% pullback since November, we propose the stock deserves a buy rating. Management seems focused, and the consumer markets for its products are growing. To paraphrase a media influencer, personal care is not an overnight process but a lifetime obsession.

Honest stock attracted attention and momentum after its second-quarter earnings set a record for quarterly revenue. Marketing Honest brand baby products and wipes drove a higher gross margin. Honest posted an unexpectedly strong Q3 ’24 earnings report that beat all estimates, sparking a run-up in the share price since last December. The next earnings report is scheduled for March 5, 2025.

Shares sold for $3.78 each at the start of November ’24, bursting to $8.97 a month later. The price hit a low of $5.87 in January and moved up to $6.39 per share through mid-February ’25. 11 of 13 analysts rate the stock a buy/strong buy and 2 tag it a hold. A Seeking Alpha editor added to the momentum by writing:

Looking ahead, The Honest Company expects full-year adjusted EBITDA of $20 million to $22 million, vs. a prior outlook of $15 million to $18 million. The forecast was well ahead of the consensus estimate of $16.5 million. On Wall Street, JPMorgan backed its Overweight rating on The Honest Company. Analyst Andrea Teixeira said that given HNST's pure-play focus in the clean and natural space, the firm views it as well positioned to benefit from the faster growth in this end market as consumers increasingly factor health, wellness, and sustainability attributes into their purchase decisions across the baby care, personal care, and home care categories.

We believe Honest’s revenue and earnings are bound to grow; the baby care and personal care industries are vibrant. The consensus among analysts is that we expect Honest to generate a profit in 2025.

The CEO’s opening statement about third quarter results to shareholders was focused; she declared the goals are to build on the momentum of “brand maximization, margin enhancement, and operating discipline…” Concomitantly, we expect the personal care and baby care products industry to grow by a CAGR of over 6%. The personal care products industry can grow at a CAGR of 7% over 7 years. Honest has plenty of room to grow.

Risk Side of Honest

However, there are risks that The Honest Company investors may encounter that can suppress stock price advancement. There are issues about tariffs on goods made in China; a plethora of Honest’s are made overseas. Insiders have to reverse their two-year-long trend of selling shares. Nearly 6M shares were sold over the past 3 months at around $6.

About 19 hedge funds, on the other hand, own shares, and increased holdings in the last quarter by acquiring over 100,000 shares at an average price of plus or minus $3.75. 6 others sold their investment as the shares about doubled. The company has an overall spotty record of EPS beating estimates.

A pesky risk that eats away at cash and reputation is legal and consumer challenges to Honest’s marketing claims. Honest claims to design, manufacture, and sell eco-clean, non-toxic, animal-cruelty free, and some vegan ingredient baby care clothing, diapers, and wipes, skincare and cleansing products for bath and beauty, makeup, hand sanitizers and alcohol hand and surface wipes. The company disputes charges in class action lawsuits and consumer complaints that were filed over the last decade contesting the quality and types of ingredients used in its products.

Seeking Alpha lowered its Quant Rating of the stock from strong buy to buy this past week. The Factor Grade for valuation dropped from B- some 6 months ago to D+ now. The metrics pulling down the grade include Honest’s EBITDA FWD which gets an F compared to the metrics of the Consumer Staples industry median sector, price/cash flow, and price/book TTM.

Company profitability metrics versus the industry’s sector medians are mixed. Honest sports a higher gross profit margin, levered FCF margin, and asset turnover ratio; however, 10 other profitability metrics fall far below the median sector metrics for Consumer Staples. Honest’s grades and metrics best those of the industry for working capital growth, Levered FCF Growth Y/Y and revenue growth (9.77% vs 1.61%) Y/Y and FWD. We are most uneasy about the company’s R&D spending which might affect challenges to its products' ingredients claims and new products that have to be constantly brought to market. FY ’24 revenue fell Y/Y from $2.07B to $2.01B. FY ’24 EPS was estimated to be -$0.07 but the actual was reported to be -$0.42.

Takeaway

It is our opinion that retail value investors with a relatively high tolerance for risk will ultimately strengthen their portfolios with a potential buy of The Honest Company stock. The stock may be overvalued by ~$2 per share, at this time, but has a strong future if management sticks to the knitting. We think it is a good value based on price-to-sales of 1.7x versus the peer average of 3.9x and Consumer Staples’ of just 1.2. The baby care and personal care industries in which Honest operates are growing and can develop life-long customers. There are risks, especially with startups. Honest is but 8 years old as a public company, yet The Honest Company was recognized among the top 100 best-led companies. Shareholder equity topped $133M. There is virtually no debt. Assets of $206M outdistance liabilities of $75M.

Other pros according to Consumer Affairs include a consumer-friendly website, fast and reliable shipping, and product variety. E-Tail claims Honest has among the Best-In-Class Digital Marketing Strategy. These factors build a sturdy foundation for growth which gets a high mark already. Though the stock’s 24-month Beta is a high 1.31, the levered/unlevered Beta over the last 12 months is a mere 0.54. Short interest has climbed to 4.3% and the valuation grade slipped as the share price climbed higher but remains below its 52-week-high.

Stocks with this valuation are not usually what we like for retail value investors, but The Honest Company does not appear to have any significant downsides that will drive down the share price. It is potentially a buying opportunity, especially on dips.

data.title: Vertiv Holdings: A Discounted Bet On AI Potential
data.description:
The Thesis

As Vertiv Holdings (NYSE:VRT) exited the year, it experienced solid growth across all three regions as demand for its power and thermal management solutions remains healthy. I expect this growth to continue further entering into 2025, as the company experienced strong order activity, primarily in its core Americas region. While the backlog levels also remain elevated near $7.2 billion as of year-end 2024, I believe the company's topline should continue to deliver double-digit growth in the quarters ahead. For margin, I think the anticipated volume growth and the company's focus on operation improvements and efficient project execution should be key drivers, leading to margin expansion in FY25 and beyond. Since my last bullish article on VRT, the stock is down in the high teens, triggered by a significant decline in AI-related stocks in January driven by the DeepSeek concern. However, as my bullish view remains intact, I still believe there is significant room for growth for the company in the coming years. Considering these factors and a significant improvement in the stock's valuation, I would recommend buying this stock at the current levels for investors with a long-term view.

Last Quarter Highlights

Following a moderate start to the year with mid-single-digit growth in the very first quarter, VRT experienced double-digit growth across its topline in the rest of FY24. Continuing this momentum, the company exited the year with yet another solid quarter, with an approximately 25.8% year on year jump in its consolidated topline to $2.35 billion in the fourth quarter of FY24. This growth was primarily a result of continued growth across all three regions, with EMEA being the strongest with nearly 32% growth driven by robust demand from its Colocation and Hyperscale customers, primarily for the company's switchgear products. Americas and APAC revenue also grew in strong double digits during the quarters, supporting the overall topline growth in the fourth quarter of FY24. For the full year 2024, the consolidated topline was up about 16.7% versus FY23, due to the impact of weak growth in the first half of the year.

Strong volume growth across the business also benefited the company’s margins during the quarter, which along with gains from favorable price cost and operational improvements led to a year-on-year growth of 380 bps in the company’s overall adjusted operating margin, reaching a record 21.5% during the quarter. Increased operating profit dollars also helped the company in its bottom line performance as its adjusted EPS nearly doubled to $0.99 from just $0.56 in the prior year's quarter, beating the consensus estimates by a notable $0.17 in the fourth quarter of 2024.

Outlook

Exiting FY24, the company reported another strong quarter with double-digit growth across all three regions. I expect this growth momentum to continue further into FY25 as the demand environment remains robust for the company products and solutions, particularly in the hyper-scale and colocation data center markets. Order growth was also strong during the quarter, primarily driven by nearly 50% growth in TTM organic orders in the Americas region. However, EMEA order growth saw weakness in the last quarter due to the project timing shift to a later period. But despite weak order in EMEA, strong growth in other regions led to a notable jump in the company’s backlog level, reaching $7.2 billion as of year-end 2024, providing significant visibility to the revenue growth in FY25.

In addition to this, the company’s Hyperscale customers are also expected to significantly increase their investments in 2025 to support infrastructure with rapidly increasing AI adoption. In my view, while the overall order activity remains robust with sustained demand across all the regions, benefit from elevated backlog levels and expected demand growth from substantial investments by customers in 2025 should drive the topline growth for the company in the quarter ahead.

Although the company is set to continue its growth further into 2025 with a robust pipeline and favorable order activity across the regions, the rapid adoption of AI and advanced workloads continues to drive the need for advanced and more complex data centers, creating opportunities for VRT for growth in the long term.

In my view, with continued densification of the data center, the requirement for advanced power and thermal management solutions to handle higher power loads and complexity in operations should also increase, acting as a significant tailwind for the company in the longer term, considering VRT’s market leadership in power and thermal management systems. I believe these rapidly increasing requirements for further advancement should continue to drive data center investments, which, along with VRT’s market position, should help the company capture a higher market share, which should result in a steady pipeline of projects for the company in the coming years.

The company has also expanded its manufacturing footprint across the United States to deal with anticipated volume growth. Also, the company is focusing on further strengthening its supply chain to address potential tariff risk and to ensure uninterrupted operation, particularly in the Americas region in the quarters ahead. In the EMEA region, while the growth is expected to be slower in 2025, the longer term remains favorable with an expected growth in AI-related investments across the region. The APAC region, on the other hand, also continues to see rapidly increasing adoption of AI in emerging markets like India and Southeast Asia, driving significant investments in data center infrastructure, which should further help the company gain market share in this high-growth region.

In my view, while the company’s expanded footprint across its primary market, Americas region, and its supply chain resilience are expected to provide a solid foundation for the region's topline growth in the coming years, VRT’s focus on gaining market share across high growth markets and its strong market position should support the company’s overall topline growth in the longer term.

Valuation Update

I last covered VRT in December 2024 maintaining my bullish view as the company continued to delivery strong growth across all the regions. However, the recent downturn across AI-related stocks, driven by DeepSeek’s announcement of a highly efficient AI model claiming to reduce hardware demand, resulted in a significant 30% decline in Vertiv's stock price possibly due to its substantial exposure to the data center end market, which is a critical part of AI related infrastructure. As a result, the company's stock is currently down by approximately 19% since my article. While the recent decline has been painful for a lot of investors, it has notably improved the stock's valuation.

Currently, the company's stock is trading at a non-GAAP forward P/E ratio of 31.07, based on its FY25 EPS estimates of $3.59. While the stock is still above its historical average multiple near 25x, it has improved notably from around 47x earnings during my last article, making the valuation extremely attractive considering its strong double-digit forward bottom line growth as can be seen in the VRT's growth table below.

Going ahead, I expect continued healthy demand for the company's solutions across all the regions, and robust order activity primarily across the core Americas region should continue to support sustained double-digit topline growth. This, along with the benefit from elevated backlog levels, should drive the company's overall topline growth in the quarters ahead. While the order activity remains favorable, VRT also continue to focus on excellent project execution and improving operational efficiency, which, along with benefits from positive price costs, should drive margin growth in FY25 and beyond. The company has also seen a decline in its interest expenses due to reduced debt levels from the prior year. In my view, as margins are set to continue expanding further, support from reduced debt burden should help the company in its bottom line expansion in the quarters ahead, further enhancing the stock's valuation in the future.

Risk

In my previous article, I highlighted the potential risk about the company's premium valuation, that it is only justified if the company continues to deliver strong growth across its top and bottom line, which also continued as evidenced by its robust Q4 results. While it is good that the recent drop in the stock price has cooled down the valuation significantly, it remains slightly above its five-year average forward multiple. Going ahead, I remain optimistic about the company's growth potential; however, the emergence of China’s efficient AI models, which claim to reduce hardware demand, poses a potential risk, particularly for the company's APAC region business. This potential headwind in one part of the business might impact the company's overall performance negatively, potentially causing underwhelming stock performance in the future.

Conclusion

As discussed above, the company stock is trading above its historical average, but has improved significantly due to the recent drop in the stock price. Continued robust demand environment across all three regions and elevated backlog levels are expected to fuel topline growth in the quarters ahead. Anticipated volume growth along with benefits from operational improvements and favorable price costs should support margin growth in FY25. Considering the company's intact long-term outlook with increasing investment for AI infrastructure enhancement, I believe the recent price correction is a compelling opportunity to bet on this strong AI player. Hence, I am maintaining my bullish view, giving VRT a "Strong Buy" rating at the current price levels.

data.title: CNA Financial: A Reliable Partner For Minority Shareholders
data.description:
CNA Financial (NYSE:CNA) is a solid insurance carrier mainly focused on providing commercial insurance coverage and covering specialty risks in the U.S. and overseas.

The company, which is owned by Loews (L) at approximately 90% is a potential pick for any dividend seekers, as the firm combines several advantages: (1) a steady underwriting performance, (2) a mid-single digit turnover growth resulting in (3) a growing dividend policy, boosted by an annual special distribution.

I have been covering CNA since 2017, and my opinion on the insurance carrier has not changed since then. The recent fourth-quarter results have proven to me that CNA remains a steady property and casualty insurance company despite the one-off effect associated with the long-term care business.

Steady 2024 Results Overshadowed By The Run-Off Business

The company reported a quarterly net income of $21 million, or a 94% drop compared to Q4 2023. The larger contributor to this earning reduction was the one-off effect from the pension settlement transaction losses, amounting to $290 million, while the property and casualty operations delivered a steady and flat performance.

In Q4 2024, the combined ratio of the property and casualty insurance division was 93.1%, or a pre-tax underwriting gain of $174 million. On a full-year basis, the non-life insurance segment recorded a combined ratio of 94.9% or a 1.4-point deterioration from 2023. It resulted in a lower FY2024 underwriting gain, which landed at $496 million pre-tax.

The margin contraction was largely driven by the higher loss ratio, which was mostly affected by higher catastrophe losses that generated 1 additional point of loss ratio than last year.

Segment-wise, all the divisions delivered a positive but reduced performance. The specialty business recorded an underwriting income of $249 million in 2024 or a 23% drop from 2023.

The specialty insurance segment suffered from lower new business, and only a 2% renewal premium change, while the loss ratio deteriorated because of continued pricing pressure in management liability lines, and the expense ratio increased slightly due to a lower earned premium base.

The commercial business observed a 6% reduction in its underwriting gains, largely driven by higher catastrophe losses. The losses from natural disasters grew from $207 million to $318 million in 2024, resulting in a loss ratio impact of 6.2 points (vs. 4.5 points in 2023).

The international business performance followed the same trend, with a 12% reduced underwriting gain to $76 million for the full year, resulting in a year-to-date combined ratio of 94%.

The underwriting margin of the overseas insurance segment was impacted by higher catastrophe losses and a higher expense ratio due to a one-off effect recorded in the prior year.

Despite the effects of the higher catastrophe losses and the higher loss trend for some portfolios, CNA has still proven that the non-life insurance business was well-performing. Going forward, CNA remains well-equipped and well-positioned to deliver a steady performance in 2025. Furthermore, the wildfire losses in California may remain absorbable, with a maximal forecasted $70 million impact for Q1 2025.

As CNA offloaded $1.045 billion in pension liabilities to MetLife (MET) in October 2024, which has been materialized by the $290 million post-tax charge during the fourth quarter, the company has accelerated its repositioning on the non-life insurance business and de-risking its portfolio by taking an immediate accounting charge.

A Steady Investment Performance

Besides the strong over-cycle performance of the property and casualty insurance business, CNA also generates recurring and steady revenues from the investment portfolio, which is mostly composed of fixed-income securities (88% of the total investment portfolio with an average credit rating of "A").

In 12M2024, the investment portfolio was valued at $47.5 billion and generated a pre-tax income of circa $2.5 billion pre-tax, or a 5.3% pre-tax return.

The higher investment income was fueled by higher income from both non-fixed and fixed-income portfolios. The higher income from the fixed-income portfolio benefited from a higher asset base, coupled with higher reinvested rates.

For 2025, CNA's management forecasts that the fixed income revenues would reach $2.23 billion:

Looking ahead to 2025, based on the current interest rate environment we expect income from fixed income and other investments to be about $550 million in the first quarter, which is about flat compared to the fourth quarter of 2024 given limited anticipated reinvestment activity. For the full year, we expect income from fixed income and other investments to be about $2,225 million, or a 2% increase as compared to the full year 2024.

If the limited partnerships and common stocks generate an income ranging from $320 to $380 million, total investment revenue could almost reach $2.6 billion pre-tax. Those higher investment revenues would fuel the potential dividend increase.

A Straightforward Dividend Policy

The company has announced that the dividend per share will increase by 4.5% to $0.46 per quarter. In addition, the insurance carrier has declared a special dividend of $2 per share, or a similar amount to last year.

At the current price (ca. $49.4 per share), the forward dividend yield is approximately 7.8% ($2 from the special dividend and the four $0.46 per share quarterly distributions or an annual payout of $3.84 per share). The capital distribution remains unchanged and serves the shareholders, minority or not, well.

Final Thoughts

Although the valuation does not appear appealing (a book value per share of $38.82 or a P/B ratio of 1.27 vs. a 5-year average of 1.08), current shareholders of the insurance carrier cannot complain. The capital allocation strategy is straightforward and transparent: serving Loews expansion, via gradually increased dividend distributions, coupled with a special dividend. Even though Loews is the majority shareholder, minority stakeholders are treated well, as they benefit from the gradually increased dividend policy, which is supported by the underwriting expertise of the insurance carrier. CNA Financial is not a company where you will double your invested capital in 2 to 5 years. However, patient dividend-oriented shareholders may enjoy being on board.

data.title: Tracking Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway Portfolio - Q4 2024 Update
data.description:
This article is part of a series that provides an ongoing analysis of the changes made to Berkshire Hathaway’s 13F stock portfolio on a quarterly basis. It is based on Warren Buffett’s regulatory 13F Form filed on 02/14/2025. Please visit our Tracking 10 Years Of Berkshire Hathaway's Investment Portfolio article series for an idea on how his holdings have progressed over the years and our previous update for the moves during Q3 2024.

During Q4 2024, Berkshire Hathaway’s (NYSE:BRK.A) (NYSE:BRK.B) 13F stock portfolio value increased from ~$266B to ~$267B. The top five positions account for ~72% of the portfolio: Apple Inc., American Express, Bank of America, CocaCola, and Chevron. There are 35 individual stock positions, many of which are small compared to the overall size of the portfolio.

Warren Buffett’s writings (pdfs) are a treasure trove of information and are a very good source for anyone starting out on individual investing. So are the classics The Intelligent Investor and Security Analysis.

Note 1: Berkshire Hathaway BYD Company (OTCPK:BYDDY) stake fell below the 5% ownership threshold during Q2 2024. Their cost-basis is ~8 HKD per share. This is compared to the current traded price in Hong Kong of ~364 HKD. Berkshire’s ownership was down from ~20% as of Q3 2022.

Note 2: It was disclosed in August 2020 that Berkshire had built ~5% stakes in five Japanese trading businesses: ITOCHU, Marubeni, Mitsubishi, Mitsui, and Sumitomo. The stakes increased to ~8.5% each during Q2 2023. The 2021 AR had the following regarding the initial purchases in three of them: 5.6% ownership stake in ITOCHU Corporation (OTCPK:ITOCF) at a cost-basis of $23.52, 5.5% ownership stake in Mitsubishi Corporation (OTCPK:MSBHF) at a cost-basis of $8.57, and 5.7% ownership stake in Mitsui & Company (OTCPK:MITSF) at a cost-basis of $8.65. ITOCF, MSBHF, and MITSF currently trade at ~$41, ~$16, and ~$17 respectively. Mitsubishi had a 3-for-1 stock split in December 2023 and Mitsui had a 2-for-1 stock split in June 2024. The cost-basis are adjusted to reflect this.

New Stakes:

Constellation Brands (STZ): STZ is a 0.47% of the portfolio position purchased this quarter at prices between ~$218 and ~$255 and the stock currently trades well below that range at ~$163.

Stake Disposals:

Ulta Beauty (ULTA): ULTA stake was established during Q2 2024 at prices between ~$376 and ~$529. The position was almost eliminated during the last quarter at prices between ~$321 and ~$413. The remainder stake was sold this quarter. The stock is now at ~$365.

SPDR S&P 500 Index (SPY), and Vanguard S&P 500 Index (VOO): These very small positions were dropped during the quarter.

Stake Increases:

Occidental Petroleum (OXY): Berkshire made a $10B investment in OXY in April 2019 through 100,000 preferred shares that has a liquidation value of $100,000 per share. Those shares pay 8% dividend, and the transaction came with warrants to purchase 83.86M shares at $59.62. In Q1 2022, Berkshire purchased ~136.4M shares at prices between ~$31 and ~$59 per share. That was followed with a ~22M share net-increase in Q2 2022 at prices between ~$57 and ~$60. The next quarter also saw a ~23% stake increase at prices between ~$57 and ~$75. The three quarters through Q2 2024 also saw a ~15% stake increase. The stock currently trades at $48.06. There was a minor ~3% stake increase this quarter.

Note: Including warrants, Berkshire owns ~36% of the business (~350M shares).

VeriSign Inc. (VRSN): VRSN was first purchased in Q4 2012 at prices between $34 and $49.50. The position was more than doubled in Q1 2013 at prices between $38 and $48. The buying continued till Q2 2014 at prices up to $63. The stock currently trades at ~$229 and the position is at ~1% of the portfolio (~13.8% of the business). There was a minor ~4% stake increase this quarter.

Sirius XM Holdings (SIRI): The stake came about as a result of a redistribution transaction by Liberty Media Group. The position was increased by ~315% during Q4 2023 as SIRI traded between ~$41 and ~$57. Q2 2024 saw another ~260% stake increase at prices between ~$25 and ~$38. That was followed by a ~12% stake increase this quarter at prices between $20.37 and $28.44. It currently trades at $27.11.

Note: in December 2023, Liberty Media announced a plan to simplify the structure - Liberty SiriusXM to combine with Sirius XM to form a new entity. The consolidation ratios were 0.8375 of new SIRI for each LSXMK/LSXMA and 0.1 of new SIRI for each SIRI held. The transaction closed during the last quarter. The prices quoted above are adjusted based on the conversion ratio.

Domino’s Pizza (DPZ): DPZ is a 0.21% of the portfolio position purchased during the last quarter at prices between ~$402 and ~$504 and the stock currently trades at ~$477. There was a ~87% stake increase this quarter at prices between ~$407 and ~$475.

Note: they have a ~7% ownership stake in the business.

Pool Corp (POOL): The very small 0.08% of the portfolio POOL stake was established during the last quarter at prices between ~$294 and ~$372 and the stock is now at ~$344. This quarter saw a ~48% stake increase at prices between ~$339 and ~$390.

Stake Decreases:

Bank of America (BAC): Berkshire established this large (top three) ~12% of the portfolio position through the exercise of Bank of America warrants. The warrants had a strike price of $7.14. Charter Communications (CHTR): CHTR is a 0.26% of the portfolio position. It was established during the last three quarters of 2014 at prices between $118 and $170. Apple (AAPL): AAPL is currently the largest 13F portfolio stake by far at ~28%. It was built between Q1 2016 and Q1 2018 at a cost-basis of ~$35 per share.
